title: 5 (+1) Tips for Starting a Solo RPG (Solo RPG Friday)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4vHxpaifn9w

The first episode of Solo RPG Friday. Daniel (@dungeondive) distils six practical tips for getting into — and staying with — solo play:

1. **Start small.** Imagination outruns logistics every time. Scaling up from a few hexes feels like progress; shrinking from a wall-sized map feels like failure.
2. **Start *in medias res*.** Stepping off a boat at the start of the *Cymbaline Isle* series was a mistake he learnt from — open on a MacGuffin or a confrontation instead.
3. **If you get stuck, cheat.** GMs flub rolls, shoehorn NPCs, and relocate dungeons constantly. Send in an eagle. The session continuing is the point.
4. **From a board-game background? Forget structured turns.** You'll need to be more fluid than you're used to.
5. **From a traditional RPG background? Add structure.** Without table banter, you'll need scaffolding to recreate the moments that stuck with you.
6. **Skip the boring downtime.** Sword-and-sorcery fiction cuts straight to the next quest hook — emulate that episodic shape rather than logging every meal.

The closing note (prompted by a Dungeon Knights patron question on staying engaged session-to-session) loops back to tip 1: starting small leaves you somewhere to expand into, which is what keeps you coming back.

----

Which of these tips landed hardest for you when you started — and is there a tip you'd add as a seventh?
